questions.prompt: In Ainsworth's Strange Situation, a securely attached infant would most likely:
options: Ignore the caregiver completely on return
Show distress when the caregiver leaves and be comforted when they return
Cling to the caregiver the entire time and refuse all exploration
Show contradictory and fearful behaviour without a clear pattern

questions.prompt: An infant who shows little outward response to the caregiver's return and tends to avoid closeness would be classified as:
Which infant pattern best matches anxious-ambivalent attachment?
options: Very clingy behaviour, distress at separation, and difficulty being comforted on reunion
No distress at departure and no interest in the caregiver's return
A balanced mix of independence and dependence

questions.prompt: Disorganized attachment is best described as:
options: A secure attachment expressed differently across cultures
A pattern involving inconsistent, contradictory, or fearful behaviour
The most common attachment style in infancy

questions.prompt: Which adult attachment style is most associated with comfort with intimacy and dependence, without excessive fear of rejection?
A person says, 'I do not like depending on others, and I prefer not to get too emotionally close.' This best matches:
A person constantly seeks reassurance, worries others do not value them as much as they value others, and craves intense emotional closeness. This best reflects:
Which adult attachment pattern is most consistent with push-pull relationships and wanting closeness while also fearing being hurt?
Which is a criticism of attachment theory discussed in lecture?
options: It pays too much attention to adulthood and not enough to childhood
It assumes categories that may oversimplify complex relational patterns
It rejects the role of caregivers entirely
It focuses only on peer relationships

questions.prompt: Which criticism best captures a limitation of classic attachment theory research?
options: It was tested equally across all cultures before making universal claims
It only applies to non-romantic relationships
It tends to claim universality despite being developed largely from one cultural context
It ignores all adult attachment findings
